0:0.rendering: The words of the Teacher, son of David, king in Jerusalem.
0:0.note: This is the voice of a man who had everything yet still wrestled with the ache of life under the sun. The Teacher points us forward to the greater Son of David, Jesus, who alone can satisfy the restless heart.

0:1.rendering: "Smoke and vapor," says the Teacher, "utter smoke and vapor! Everything is smoke and vapor."
0:1.note: The Hebrew word hevel, repeated here like a sigh, means the breath you see on a cold morning—fleeting, insubstantial, gone in a moment. Qoheleth stares at life under the sun and calls it exactly what it is, so we won't pin our hopes on what can't hold them. This honest ache prepares the heart for the One who stepped into our vapor to give us what lasts forever.

0:2.rendering: What real gain does a person have from all the hard work they pour themselves into under the sun?
0:2.note: The Hebrew word for "gain" (yitron) carries the quiet ache of asking whether anything we sweat for actually lasts. Solomon stares at the treadmill of human effort and finds it empty—until we lift our eyes to the One who worked the hardest of all on a cross, then rose to give us a profit that death itself cannot touch.

0:3.rendering: One generation goes, and another comes, but the earth stays put forever.
0:3.note: Life feels like an endless parade of faces coming and going while the ground beneath us never budges. In the middle of that weary cycle, Jesus steps in as the One who is the same yesterday, today, and forever, anchoring our restless hearts to something that truly lasts.

0:4.rendering: The sun rises and the sun sets, then it hurries back to the place where it rises again.
0:4.note: This simple rhythm of the sun reminds us that the world keeps turning in its weary cycles, yet the One who made the sun stepped into our time to bring us rest that never wears out.

0:5.rendering: The wind blows to the south, then swirls back to the north; it spins and spins on its circuits, and the wind keeps returning on its rounds.
0:5.note: Like that restless wind chasing its own tail, we often spin in the same old ruts. Jesus invites us into His rest—the One who calmed the winds and whose Spirit blows where He wills, breaking the cycle of weary striving.

0:6.rendering: All the streams flow into the sea, yet the sea is never full. To the place where the streams flow, there they keep returning.
0:6.note: The Hebrew word for "streams" (nachal) carries the picture of a torrent that never quits its course. This verse shows the relentless, weary cycle of nature that leaves us unsatisfied. Only Jesus, the living water, can break the endless loop and fill the empty sea of our hearts.

0:7.rendering: All things are wearying; no one can put it into words. The eye is never satisfied with seeing, nor the ear filled with hearing.
0:7.note: The Preacher felt the bone-deep exhaustion of a world that keeps repeating itself without ever quite satisfying the soul. Jesus steps into that very weariness and says, “Come to me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest” (Matt 11:28). In him the restless heart finally finds the One who fills the eye and ear forever.

0:8.rendering: What has been is what will be, and what has been done is what will be done; there is nothing new under the sun.
0:8.note: The Preacher stares at the weary cycles of life and reminds us that chasing novelty apart from God is empty. Yet this same honest voice prepares our hearts for the One who makes all things new in Christ.

0:9.rendering: Is there anything of which one can say, "Look, this is brand new"? It has already existed long ago, before our time.
0:9.note: The Preacher cuts through our hunger for novelty with a single honest question. What feels fresh to us is usually just the same old ache wearing new clothes. Yet the One who makes all things new has stepped into our weary cycle, offering a hope that truly breaks the endless loop.

0:10.rendering: There is no lasting memory of those who came before us, and those who come after will not be remembered by those who follow them.
0:10.note: The Teacher stares straight into our hunger for legacy and says it slips away like sand through fingers. In a world that forgets so quickly, Jesus steps in as the One whose name will never be erased, inviting us to find our lasting place hidden in Him rather than in our own fleeting fame.

0:11.rendering: I, the Teacher, was king over Israel in Jerusalem.
0:11.note: The word for “Teacher” (Qoheleth) carries the weight of one who gathers and assembles people to hear hard truth. Even the richest, wisest king who ever lived looked at life under the sun and found it maddeningly empty; only Jesus, the greater Son of David, can fill that hollow place in our chests.

0:12.rendering: I devoted myself with all my heart to explore and examine by wisdom everything that is done under the sun. What a miserable task God has given to the children of man to keep them busy!
0:12.note: The Hebrew word for "search" here carries the weight of a relentless, almost painful investigation—like a man digging through the rubble of life looking for answers that keep slipping away. In our restless quest for meaning, we meet the One who searched the depths of our sin and suffering on the cross, so that our restless hearts could finally find their rest in him.

0:13.rendering: I have seen all the deeds that are done under the sun, and look—everything is vapor, a chasing after wind.
0:13.note: The Preacher stares honestly at life and calls it what it is: fleeting and frustrating. Only when we bring our restless hearts to Jesus, the One who entered our vapor-world to redeem it, does the chase finally find its rest.

0:14.rendering: What is crooked cannot be straightened, and what is lacking cannot be counted.
0:14.note: The Teacher stares at the twisted places in our world and in our own lives and says plainly: some damage runs too deep for human hands to fix. Only the One who hung crooked on a tree for us can make the bent straight again.

0:15.rendering: I said to myself, "Look, I have grown and increased in wisdom far beyond anyone who ruled over Jerusalem before me; my mind has absorbed vast wisdom and knowledge."
0:15.note: Qoheleth's relentless quest for wisdom under the sun left him empty-handed, for the fear of the Lord is the true beginning of knowledge. This honest confession ultimately drives us to Jesus, who is himself the wisdom of God and the only One who can satisfy the restless human heart.

0:16.rendering: So I set my heart to know wisdom and knowledge, madness and folly; I saw that this too was chasing after wind.
0:16.note: The Teacher threw himself into the quest for wisdom with everything he had, only to discover it couldn't fill the hollow place inside. That ache points us to Jesus, the Wisdom of God who doesn't just teach us truth but becomes our peace when all our striving leaves us empty.

0:17.rendering: Because the more wisdom you pile up, the more pain you carry; the more understanding you gain, the more sorrow you feel.
0:17.note: This verse stings with honesty. Solomon, who had more insight than anyone, discovered that wisdom without God only sharpens our awareness of the world's brokenness. True wisdom finds its rest in Christ, who was acquainted with grief yet invites us to trade our heavy knowledge for his easy yoke.
